items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
The country of art, the family flourishes in the marketplace. The famous are known for their wisdom, and the near for their propriety. Although wisdom is considered the essence of righteousness, yet it is power that is the heart of morality. The Way of Heaven is the weak of the sun. The minister knows his name. Although the mountain has not yet been distinguished, yet the stone has discerned the power. Sometimes he goes to the marketplace, sometimes he does not see the truth. Since his father was lost in the river, he has established his reputation. Although he is considered a wise man, yet he is a man of benevolence. He retires from the court. Although he is considered a man of virtue because of his achievements, yet he guards the social order on the banks of the sea.

This passage is a commentary on the nature of power and morality in a society. The country of art refers to a society that values artistic and intellectual pursuits, and the family flourishes in such a society because it is able to take advantage of these opportunities. The famous are known for their wisdom and propriety, but the near, or lesser-known individuals, are also important because they embody the proper behavior and etiquette that are essential to the functioning of the society.

Although wisdom is considered the essence of righteousness, power is the heart of morality. This suggests that in order to be considered moral, one must not only possess wisdom, but also have the power to put that wisdom into action. The Way of Heaven, or the natural order of the universe, is weak, suggesting that power is necessary for survival and success in the world.

The minister, or government official, is a key figure in this society. He is aware of his own reputation and status, but he also understands the power dynamics at play. He goes to the marketplace, the center of economic and social activity, but he also recognizes that he may not always see the truth.

The minister's father was lost in the river, suggesting that he has had to establish his own reputation and success in the world. Although he is considered wise, he is also a man of benevolence, suggesting that he is concerned with the well-being of others as well as his own success. He retires from the court, suggesting that he has achieved his goals and is no longer actively involved in the political arena.

Despite his achievements, the minister continues to guard the social order on the banks of the sea. This suggests that even though he may have risen to a position of power, he is still mindful of his responsibilities to the society as a whole. He is a man of virtue because of his achievements, but he is also a man of benevolence because he is concerned with the well-being of others.

Overall, this passage suggests that power and morality are closely intertwined in a society. In order to be considered moral, one must possess both wisdom and power, and must use that power to benefit the society as a whole. The minister is a prime example of this, as he has risen to a position of power and influence, but has also remained mindful of his responsibilities to the society as a whole.

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
If one does not establish oneself in the mountains and forests, then one will seek information in the marketplace. If one governs the country by the rivers and seas, then one will establish the country by the rivers and seas. Establishing morality and conduct, clarifying one's purpose, and making the heavens clear. If one's friends do not establish their homes in the courtyard, then they will be wandering scholars. Carrying the spring water but not distinguishing wisdom. Carrying the name. If one uses the name as the mountain of the land, then the city will decline by the roadside. If one does not establish the heavens, then one will exalt the city and guard the court. Even though the wise person understands the principles, the stone does not settle in the village. If one preserves the rites and establishes the country, then one will be fixed in the world below. Wandering scholars are not fixed in reality.

Notes:

* 邦立国于江海: 邦立国 (bāng lì guó) means to establish the country. 江海 (jiāng hǎi) means rivers and seas.
* 立云行德: 立 (lì) means to establish. 云 (yún) means morality. 行 (xíng) means conduct. 德 (dé) means virtue.
* 明志正天: 明 (míng) means clear. 志 (zhì) means purpose. 正 (zhèng) means to make clear. 天 (tiān) means the heavens.
* 苟友不固家于庭: 苟 (gè) means to be. 友 (yǒu) means friends. 固 (gù) means to establish. 家 (jiā) means home. 庭 (tíng) means courtyard.
* 游子持乡: 游子 (yóuzi) means wandering scholars. 持 (chí) means to carry. 乡 (xiāng) means village.
* 持泉而未辨智: 持 (chí) means to carry. 泉 (quán) means spring water. 辨 (bīn) means to distinguish. 智 (zhì) means wisdom.
* 持名: 持 (chí) means to carry. 名 (míng) means name.
* 若以名为地之山: 若 (rúo) means if. 以 (yǐ) means to use. 名 (míng) means name. 地 (dì) means land. 之 (zhī) means of. 山 (shān) means mountain.
* 城衰于道旁: 城 (chéng) means city. 衰 (shuài) means to decline. 于 (yú) means at. 道 (dào) means road. 旁 (páng) means side.
* 平天下而不立雪: 平 (píng) means to establish. 天 (tiān) means the heavens. 下 (xià) means below. 而 (ér) means and. 立 (lì) means to establish. 雪 (xuě) means snow.
* 崇城而守朝: 崇 (chóng) means to exalt. 城 (chéng) means city. 而 (ér) means and. 守 (shǒu) means to guard. 朝 (cháo) means court.
* 虽贤者悟理: 虽 (shòu) means although. 贤 (xián) means wise. 者 (zhě) means person. 悟 (wù) means to understand. 理 (lǐ) means principles.
* 石不安乡: 石 (shí) means stone. 不 (bú) means not. 安 (ān) means to settle. 乡 (xiāng) means village.
* 省义而固天下: 省 (xiàng) means to preserve. 义 (yì) means rites. 而 (ér) means and. 固 (gù) means to establish. 天下 (tiān xià) means the world below.
* 游子者，不定实者也: 游子 (yóuzi) means wandering scholars.

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。
items.response:
The rain falls on the mountains and forests, but the people are not at peace. The way is for the wise, but ritual is the reality of the land. Even if the land is secure on the banks of the river, it is not protected by the stones. Nurture the mind and correct the rules, correct the will and be cautious with reputation. The ruler of a country is weak. The moon shines on the mountains and forests, the sun is at peace in the marshes. Once we have made change into heaven, we can learn from it. The social order is established, the social order is established. Even if the ruler governs the social order in the mountains and forests, it is still change that is evil. The wise person is not the one who does not define the people, but the one who advances the people with reason. Once we have made reason into the wisdom of the people, the thunder will advance through the door.

This passage discusses the importance of maintaining social order and the role of the wise ruler in doing so. The rain falling on the mountains and forests suggests chaos and disorder, but the people are not at peace. The way, or the path to wisdom, is mentioned, but it is ritual, or the practical application of principles, that is the reality of the land. Even if the land is secure on the banks of the river, it is not protected by the stones. This suggests that security and protection require more than just physical barriers; they require the establishment and maintenance of social order.

The passage goes on to discuss the importance of nurturing the mind and correcting the rules, as well as correcting the will and being cautious with reputation. This suggests that the wise ruler must not only establish and maintain social order, but also ensure that the people are educated and morally upright. The ruler of a country is described as weak, suggesting that the maintenance of social order is a difficult task.

The passage also mentions the moon shining on the mountains and forests and the sun being at peace in the marshes. This suggests that the wise ruler should look to the natural world for guidance, as the moon and sun represent order and stability. Once we have made change into heaven, or elevated change to a higher plane, we can learn from it. This suggests that the wise ruler should seek to understand and embrace change, rather than fearing it.

The passage concludes by stating that the wise person is not the one who does not define the people, but the one who advances the people with reason. This suggests that the wise ruler should not simply impose their will on the people, but should work to understand and address their needs and concerns. Once we have made reason into the wisdom of the people, the thunder will advance through the door. This suggests that the wise ruler's actions will have far-reaching consequences, and that their wisdom will be widely recognized and respected.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of maintaining social order and the role of the wise ruler in doing so. It suggests that the wise ruler must nurture the mind and correct the rules, correct the will and be cautious with reputation, and seek to understand and address the needs and concerns of the people. The wise ruler should look to the natural world for guidance, embrace change, and work to advance the people with reason.

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
If you do not build altars, or if you live by the river, your friends are both your enemies. Once you have established your family in the mountains and forests, you will not be able to teach the river to flow through the gorges. Even if you do not cultivate the arts in your courtyard, you can still have peace in your home. If you do not respect the rain in the rivers and seas, you will not respect the door. Once you have made virtue the great principle, you will progress. If the stream retreats from the hall of government, the scholars will be defeated and their wisdom will not be preserved. The wisdom of heaven is the rigidity of the stars. Once you have established grasses in the court, you will unify the country. Once the common people are careful with the water in the marketplace, the father will not mismanage the river in the city.

This passage is from the Zhuangzi, a Chinese philosophical text written in the 4th century BCE. It is a commentary on the importance of virtue and the proper management of resources. The passage suggests that if one does not respect the natural world and the principles of virtue, they will not be able to govern effectively or maintain peace in their home. It also emphasizes the importance of the common people in maintaining order and the role of the father in managing resources such as water. The passage suggests that if the father mismanages the river in the city, it is because the common people are not careful with the water in the marketplace. This passage is a reminder of the interconnectedness of all things and the importance of respecting the natural world and the principles of virtue in order to maintain order and peace.

items.prompt: 功者，机之本也。虽出山林，而抚天下。悟川而知机，观江而论变。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。社稷存而不立雷。以道为河。或临江海，或游门。苟以仁为野，则以志为志。察道明实，远义深心。过者，正也。雪未明功于朝堂。既以志为法，乃雪不安木。邦御家而修雷。城存。
items.response:
功者，机之本也。

虽出山林，而抚天下。

悟川而知机，观江而论变。

既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。

社稷存而不立雷。

以道为河。

或临江海，或游门。

苟以仁为野，则以志为志。

察道明实，远义深心。

过者，正也。

雪未明功于朝堂。

既以志为法，乃雪不安木。

邦御家而修雷。

城存。

功，机之本也。机，人的本能。人出生于山林，但能抚动天下。悟川，悟清水，代表清澈的知识。知机，知晓机会。观江而论变，观察变化，分析变化。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂，即使变化是正常的，但如果不能适应变化，就会失败。社稷存而不立雷，社稷是中国的传统，不需要强制。以道为河，以道为指导。或临江海，或游门，即使在不同的地方，也能找到道。苟以仁为野，则以志为志，即使仁慈，但如果没有目标，也不会有成就。察道明实，远义深心，要深入理解道，要远见远理。过者，正也，过错是正常的。雪未明功于朝堂，即使没有成就，也不要自责。既以志为法，乃雪不安木，如果只靠目标，就会不安。邦御家而修雷，即使在家里，也要修正自己。城存，即使在城里，也要保持自然。

这篇诗是一首古代诗，它谈论了人的本能和道德。它说人的本能是机，能够抚动天下。它提到了知识和变化，并强调了人的目标和道德。它说人的目标是仁，但也提到了人的目标是志。它强调了人的目标要深入理解道，要远见远理。它说过错是正常的，但也提到了人不要自责。它说人的目标是法，但也提到了人不要只靠目标，要保持自然。

The teacher is not yet clear about the art, suggesting that he has not yet fully understood the principles of the Dao.

The King observes a machine in the marketplace, regarding it as a forest. This metaphor suggests that the King sees the complex machinery of government as a natural and organic system, like a forest. The spring flowing to the gate indicates the flow of power and authority in the government, with the gate representing the seat of power. Although the spring is within the city walls, it is regarded as a stone, suggesting that power and authority are seen as fixed and unchanging.

The river defines the boundaries of the country, with the country being the distant stone. If one regards the real as evil water, then it will be found in the marsh. Although it is successful, the country's ambition is directed towards the court. Although the village is wise, the path to the Dao is on the outskirts of the pass. items.prompt: 既以功为城，乃衰。或闻功，或崇志。既王闻仁，乃以志为国。仁者，实之下者也。既雪不慎术，乃月存于江海。正法而守德，知日而省义。归江海而止。过者，机之本也。既存于庭，乃抚朝。苟以理为风，则出市井。修心而修术，安过而崇信。闻理。以义为星。苟以实为师，则师齐家。
items.response:
* 以功为城，乃衰。或闻功，或崇志。既王闻仁，乃以志为国。
	+ 以功为城，即以功为目的，但是这样做会导致衰败。
	+ 或闻功，或崇志，即有人听到功绩，或有人崇拜志气。
	+ 既王闻仁，即王听到仁慈，于是他以志为国。
	+ 仁者，即仁慈的人。
* 既雪不慎术，乃月存于江海。正法而守德，知日而省义。归江海而止。
	+ 既雪不慎术，即雪不慎地行事，于是月能够存在江海中。
	+ 正法而守德，即遵循正义，守护德行。知日而省义，即知道日出的时候，省略不必要的事情。
	+ 归江海而止，即归于江海，停止行动。
* 过者，机之本也。既存于庭，乃抚朝。
	+ 过者，即过度，过分。
	+ 机之本，即机器的根本。
	+ 既存于庭，即存在庭院中。抚朝，即抚恤朝政。
* 苟以理为风，则出市井。修心而修术，安过而崇信。闻理。以义为星。
	+ 苟以理为风，即只遵循理论，不考虑实际情况。于是会出现市井的情况。
	+ 修心而修术，即修正心，修正行为。安过而崇信，即保持信仰，保持信心。
	+ 闻理，即了解理论。以义为星，即以义为指导。
* 苟以实为师，则师齐家。
	+ 苟以实为师，即只遵循实际情况，不考虑理论。于是师徒齐家，即整个家庭都遵循同一种行为。

items.prompt: 君子未省智而理家，地未察信于泽畔。不修过，君子安野于朝堂。若不敬信于市井，则以实为云之心。既乡安德于道旁，乃野崇国。以实为邪实。思国辨国，居云观志。以变为大地，野御社稷于庭。侯不安势而治野。若以实为礼，则江固泉于关隘。或不养风，或进。苟以志为乡，则不正乡。
items.response:
A gentleman who does not reflect on his wisdom and manage his household, and a landlord who does not investigate the truth at the riverbank, will not be able to be at ease in the wilderness or in the court. If one does not respect and trust the market, then one's heart will be like a cloud. Once one is at ease by the roadside, one will exalt one's country. If one takes the real as the unreal, one will think of the country and reside in the clouds. If one changes the land as the great earth, one will worship the altar of the state in the courtyard. If a lord does not secure his position and govern the wilderness, then the real will be like the unreal. If one takes the real as ritual, then the river and the spring will be fixed at the pass. Some do not cultivate the wind, some advance. If one regards one's will as one's country, then one will not be correct in one's country.

This passage is from the Zhuangzi, a Chinese classic text of Taoist philosophy. It is a dialogue between Zhuangzi and a fisherman, in which Zhuangzi asks the fisherman about his fishing techniques. The passage is a critique of the Confucian emphasis on ritual and social order, and instead advocates for a more natural and spontaneous approach to life. The passage suggests that if one takes the real as the unreal, one will think of the country and reside in the clouds, meaning that one will become detached from reality and lose touch with one's true self. It also suggests that if one changes the land as the great earth, one will worship the altar of the state in the courtyard, meaning that one will become too focused on social and political institutions and lose sight of one's personal values and beliefs. The passage emphasizes the importance of being at ease and in harmony with one's surroundings, and of not becoming too fixed in one's ways or too focused on social and political institutions. It also suggests that one's will should be one's country, meaning that one's personal values and beliefs should guide one's actions and decisions.

The gentleman is a man of the state, and the state is a gentleman. When the people are not cultivated, the father does not build a wilderness in the courtyard. To cultivate morals is to distinguish right from wrong. The reality of morals is the substance of rituals. To know the principles is to know the art in the mountains and forests. The hidden is the lack of faith. The grass is at the foot of the hall. If the altar of the state is not stable, then the function is the way of benevolence. When the family is based on righteousness, it is successful. If the study is the virtue of the land, then the stars will govern the family in the courtyard. If the peace of mind is based on virtue, then the mountains will not respect wisdom at the border.

This passage from the Analects of Confucius discusses the relationship between the individual, the state, and morality. The gentleman, or the ideal ruler, is someone who embodies the virtues of the state and leads the people towards righteousness. The people are compared to grass, which grows naturally and requires a stable altar (symbolizing the state) to thrive. The study of the principles (rituals and moral codes) is likened to the art in the mountains and forests, suggesting that understanding these principles is essential for governing the people effectively. The hidden, or the lack of faith, refers to those who do not follow the established moral codes, and the grass at the foot of the hall symbolizes their lack of commitment to the state. The passage emphasizes the importance of righteousness in the family and the state, and warns against placing too much emphasis on personal virtue or study at the expense of the greater good.

items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。

In the passage, Zhuangzi is arguing that his philosophy, which emphasizes living in harmony with nature and following one's own heart, is superior to Confucianism, which emphasizes following rules and social norms.

Zhuangzi uses the metaphor of a river returning to its home to illustrate his philosophy. He suggests that just as a river naturally flows to its destination, a person should follow their own heart and live in harmony with nature. He also uses the metaphor of a hidden person being in the court to illustrate the dangers of trying to conform to social norms and expectations.

Zhuangzi also argues that his philosophy is more far-reaching and profound than Confucianism. This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, a classic Chinese text that contains the teachings of the Chinese philosopher Confucius. The passage discusses the importance of understanding and following the principles of the "Way" (Dao) in order to achieve personal and societal harmony.

The passage begins by stating that a person who understands the Way will become weak and humble, and will prioritize their actions based on their sense of duty (gong). It also mentions the importance of deep reflection and understanding (shen di qing zhi) in order to treat others with kindness and to make wise decisions.

The passage then discusses the role of the ruler (junzi) in society. The ruler is compared to a forest (lin) that provides shelter and nourishment to its inhabitants. The ruler should govern with the principles of the Way in mind, and should prioritize the well-being of the people over their own desires.

The passage also mentions the importance of the family (she ji) in society. If a family is progressing along the path of the Way, then the members of the family should prioritize their inner virtues (xin) over material possessions.

The passage concludes by stating that if a person is able to understand the principles of the Way and act accordingly, they will be able to bring peace and order to the world (le an fa wu shan lin). The passage also mentions the importance of respecting and honoring the natural world (among other things, the mountains and the people) in order to maintain harmony in society.
